summary: Joel announces that Area 51, the community-driven platform for proposing new Stack Exchange sites, has entered beta. He addresses a Hacker News commenter's criticism that the site seems overly complicated and questions its business model. Joel humorously deflects the business model question with a Saturday Night Live reference. He draws a parallel to early skeptics of Stack Overflow itself, who predicted it wouldn't last a year, and notes that Area 51 is intentionally designed for committed users rather than casual visitors.
key_points: Area 51 launched in beta as the community-driven process for creating new Stack Exchange sites
Early critics dismissed Stack Overflow's UI and predicted it wouldn't survive a year, proving spectacularly wrong
Products designed for committed communities don't need to be immediately accessible to everyone
Joel deflects the monetization question with humor rather than a serious answer
money_quotes: I'm not one to take Internet chat board comments seriously.
If you don't know what it's for, or why it's going to work, or you can't figure it out, it's not, actually for you.
key_insight: Products built for committed communities can afford to be complex and uninviting to casual visitors — early skepticism from outsiders is often a sign you're building for the right audience.
